bins.name: Raise threshold to 100 MW
bins.desc: Crusoe argues the proposed 20 MW threshold is too low and would flood the queue with smaller projects better handled through existing processes. It recommends a 100 MW floor so that streamlined federal rules target only the largest, highest-impact facilities like Crusoe's own 1,200 MW projects.
quotes: The proposed 20 MW threshold for the new large load and hybrid facility interconnections may be too low to efficiently advance AI deployment.  The Commission should consider raising the threshold to facilities above 100 MW.

streamlined processes should prioritize large facilities above 100 MW.  For example, Crusoe has under development facilities in the 1,200 MW range with both grid interconnection and onsite natural gas production.

bins.name: Joint studies for hybrid facilities
bins.desc: Crusoe supports studying load and generation together for hybrid facilities. Joint studies produce a more accurate picture of net grid impact and reduce the need for costly network upgrades.
quotes: Joint interconnection studies for hybrid facilities that bring new generation with load will expedite the interconnection process and minimize the need for costly and time-consuming construction of network upgrades because the impact of the new load can be offset by the addition of new generation.

Hybrid facility structures cause significantly less impact on the transmission grid than large loads that do not bring generation.  Thus, where hybrid facilities are proposed, the load and generation should be studied together to get an accurate picture of the load's impact on the grid.

bins.name: Higher deposits to curb speculation
bins.desc: Crusoe backs sharply higher deposit fees (at least $100,000) and mandatory site control to block speculative queue entries. It also wants objective technical and financial criteria, not just qualitative curtailment pledges, so queue positions reflect real development intent.
quotes: Speculative interconnection requests are hindering AI deployment across the country.  Speculative requests will continue to delay interconnection for large scale projects and lead to uncertainty in real load demand.

Crusoe supports the comments of Google and OpenAI asking the Commission to impose heightened standards for interconnection requests, including significant increase in deposit fees and site control requirements to deter speculative requests.

The Commission should instead establish clear, objective criteria that require interconnection customers to demonstrate both technical capability and financial commitment beyond mere qualitative assurances of curtailment flexibility.
Such qualification standards can include site control requirements, minimum queue fees of $100,000 or greater, and tendering full security for transmission upgrades.

bins.name: Prioritize hybrid facilities
bins.desc: Hybrid facilities that bring onsite generation impose less grid burden than pure load and should be studied on the basis of actual injection and withdrawal rights. Crusoe urges FERC to treat hybrids as a distinct, preferred category.
quotes: hybrid facilities combining interconnection requests with onsite generation should be prioritized as they can reduce load requirements while potentially supporting the grid during peak demand.

Hybrid facility structures cause significantly less impact on the transmission grid than large loads that do not bring generation.  Thus, where hybrid facilities are proposed, the load and generation should be studied together to get an accurate picture of the load's impact on the grid.

bins.name: Protection facilities for hybrids
bins.desc: Crusoe supports mandatory system protection facilities for hybrid interconnections to prevent injections or withdrawals that exceed contracted rights, with minimum telemetry and operating standards.
quotes: The Commission should require hybrid interconnections to install the system protection facilities necessary to prevent unauthorized injections or withdrawals that exceed the respective rights available to the interconnection customer.

bins.name: Expedited process requires proven flexibility
bins.desc: Crusoe favors fast-track interconnection for curtailable loads but insists that applicants must prove actual technical curtailment ability. Giving expedited treatment to unproven technologies would create new inefficiencies.
quotes: it is critical that companies must demonstrate actual technical ability to curtail loads and advance flexible computing to be eligible for expedited interconnection.  If there are no stringent requirements for flexible load, the Commission could inadvertently create inefficiencies if unproven technologies are prioritized and expedited.

bins.name: Study reforms must address speculation
bins.desc: Crusoe supports study-process reforms but ties their effectiveness to removing speculative queue entries first. Accurate load data is a prerequisite for reliable interconnection studies.
quotes: Joint interconnection studies for hybrid facilities that bring new generation with load will expedite the interconnection process and minimize the need for costly and time-consuming construction of network upgrades because the impact of the new load can be offset by the addition of new generation.

Speculative interconnection requests are hindering AI deployment across the country.  Speculative requests will continue to delay interconnection for large scale projects and lead to uncertainty in real load demand.

bins.name: Co-location reduces grid burden
bins.desc: Crusoe argues that co-locating generation with load materially reduces transmission grid impact and should be recognized in both the study process and the allocation of interconnection costs.
quotes: hybrid facilities combining interconnection requests with onsite generation should be prioritized as they can reduce load requirements while potentially supporting the grid during peak demand.

Joint interconnection studies for hybrid facilities that bring new generation with load will expedite the interconnection process and minimize the need for costly and time-consuming construction of network upgrades because the impact of the new load can be offset by the addition of new generation.

Hybrid facility structures cause significantly less impact on the transmission grid than large loads that do not bring generation.  Thus, where hybrid facilities are proposed, the load and generation should be studied together to get an accurate picture of the load's impact on the grid.

bins.name: Flexibility must be demonstrated
bins.desc: Crusoe supports flexible load incentives but insists that eligibility for expedited treatment should require demonstrated curtailment capability, not just qualitative commitments.
quotes: streamlined processes should prioritize large facilities above 100 MW.  For example, Crusoe has under development facilities in the 1,200 MW range with both grid interconnection and onsite natural gas production.

The Commission should instead establish clear, objective criteria that require interconnection customers to demonstrate both technical capability and financial commitment beyond mere qualitative assurances of curtailment flexibility.

it is critical that companies must demonstrate actual technical ability to curtail loads and advance flexible computing to be eligible for expedited interconnection.  If there are no stringent requirements for flexible load, the Commission could inadvertently create inefficiencies if unproven technologies are prioritized and expedited.

bins.name: Hybrid facilities have lower grid impact
bins.desc: Because onsite generation offsets withdrawal, hybrid facilities have a smaller footprint on the transmission grid than standalone large loads. Crusoe asks FERC to reflect this difference in study methodology and cost allocation.
quotes: Hybrid facility structures cause significantly less impact on the transmission grid than large loads that do not bring generation.  Thus, where hybrid facilities are proposed, the load and generation should be studied together to get an accurate picture of the load's impact on the grid.

bins.name: Cost allocation based on grid use
bins.desc: Transmission and ancillary service charges should be calculated on the net power actually withdrawn from the grid, not on total facility consumption. Crusoe cites ERCOT's generation-netting settlement as a model.
quotes: Large loads and hybrid facilities should thus pay their fair share of current and future grid costs based on their use of the grid.

bins.name: SPP HILL/HILLGA model endorsed
bins.desc: Crusoe explicitly endorses SPP's High Impact Large Load and HILLGA framework as a proactive, stakeholder-backed approach that protects resource adequacy while accommodating large load growth.
quotes: Crusoe supports SPP's currently proposed High Impact Large Load (HILL) and High Impact Large Load Generation Assessment (HILLGA) model because it is proactive and supported by a broad group of industry stakeholders.

bins.name: No retroactive rule application
bins.desc: Crusoe opposes applying new interconnection rules to projects already in the queue. Retroactive changes force re-studies, extend timelines, and have already delayed Crusoe's own voltage ride-through approvals.
quotes: subsequent rule or process changes should not apply to pending interconnection requests retroactively.  Crusoe is concerned that projects can get caught in a never-ending spiral of rule changes that will cause considerable delays even for projects that are nearly finished with the interconnection process.

bins.name: Equipment procurement bottleneck
bins.desc: Even with faster studies, transformer and switchgear lead times of up to 48 months will remain the critical path for large load projects. Crusoe urges coordinated federal policy to strengthen domestic manufacturing capacity for electrical equipment.
quotes: supply chain constraints for gas and electrical long lead equipment represent a separate challenge that will require complementary policy solutions.  Even with expedited interconnection studies, the time required to manufacture, deliver, and energize network upgrades and new transmission facilities will continue to create long lead times before large loads can be energized.

bins.name: Eliminate PUHCA sleeve PPA rule
bins.desc: Crusoe supports eliminating the requirement to use sleeve PPAs to preserve PUHCA exemptions in states that permit direct retail sales. The sleeve requirement adds cost and delay without operational value.
quotes: By requiring sleeve PPAs in states that would otherwise allow retail sales to large loads, the PUHCA regulations create significant impediments to hybrid facility development and increase costs.
Eliminating the sleeve requirement to maintain PUHCA exemptions for upstream owners would reduce costs and accelerate project development.
